common_name: Yellow Passion Fruit (Golden Passion Fruit, Maracujá Amarelo)

planting.note: Start seed indoors 8–10 weeks before planting out; transplant only after all frost risk has passed and soil is warm above 65°F; in frost-free zones, direct sow after the wet season begins.

directions: Sow seed just below the surface in warm starting mix and maintain 75–85°F for germination; yellow passion fruit is the world's most commercially cultivated passion fruit, producing golf-ball- to lemon-sized yellow-skinned fruits with intensely fragrant, tart orange-yellow pulp that is the basis of most commercial passion fruit juice. A rampant perennial vine to 30 feet, it needs a very sturdy trellis or fence and frost-free conditions; train two or three main canes horizontally along wires and allow laterals to cascade. Flowers require cross-pollination between two genetically distinct plants, or hand-pollination outside the tropics where native bee pollinators are absent. Harvest when fruit begins to show yellow color and drops easily from the vine.

planting.note: Start seed indoors 10–12 weeks before last frost; alternately, sow fresh ripe seed directly in late summer — blue passionflower germinates most reliably from very fresh seed; established plants die back to roots in zones 7–8 and resprout vigorously in spring.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun and provide a trellis or fence for the vigorous tendrils; blue passionflower is the most cold-hardy of the fruiting passion vines, tolerating brief dips to 10–15°F when established roots are protected by mulch. The intricate blue, white, and purple flowers with their alien-looking filament corona rank among the most striking summer blooms of any garden vine, followed by oval orange-yellow fruits that are edible but bland compared to commercial passion fruit — grow it primarily for the spectacular flowers and cold hardiness. Dies to the ground in zones 7–8 but regrows strongly from deep roots; do not remove dormant crowns too early in spring.

planting.note: Plant only in frost-free gardens or large containers moved under cover in winter; requires a very robust support structure — mature vines and their heavy fruits demand strong anchoring.

directions: Plant in deep, rich, well-drained soil in full sun with an extremely sturdy support capable of handling 8-pound fruits; giant granadilla produces the world's largest passion fruits — 6 to 12 inches long and weighing up to 8 pounds — with whitish-yellow skin, a thick edible rind used as a vegetable, and seedy interior pulp that makes refreshing juice and drinks popular across tropical Latin America. The vine is extraordinarily vigorous, growing 30–50 feet annually, and requires rigorous pruning after harvest. Flowers measure 4–5 inches across with a remarkable corona of purple, white, and pink filaments. In Central America, the unripe fruit rind is cooked as a vegetable in soups and stews; the ripe pulp is eaten fresh or juiced.

planting.note: Thrives in cool tropical highland conditions at 60–75°F; not suited to hot, humid lowland tropics where flavor and sugar content diminish — plant at elevation where temperatures are mild.

directions: Plant in fertile, moist but well-drained soil in full sun with wire or trellis support; sweet granadilla is widely considered the sweetest and most delicately flavored of all passion fruits, with a brittle hard shell that rattles when ripe and a translucent sweet-gelatinous interior pulp eaten with a spoon — the dominant commercial species in Colombia, Peru, and Venezuela. It differs from P. flavicarpa in preferring cool highland conditions (3,000–8,000 feet elevation in the tropics, or mild Mediterranean climates); excessive heat reduces sugar content significantly. Harvest when the shell becomes brittle and the fruit sounds hollow when tapped. A single established vine on a strong trellis can yield 100 or more fruits per season.

planting.note: Listed as an invasive species in Hawaii and New Zealand — check local regulations before planting; plant in mild coastal or mountain climates; tolerates light frost better than most passion fruits.

directions: Plant in rich, moist, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade with trellis or fence support; banana passion fruit produces elongated pale yellow-green fruits 2–4 inches long with a strong passion fruit flavor and abundant aromatic juice used in drinks and ice cream throughout the Colombian highlands, where it is called curuba. The soft-hairy three-lobed leaves and pendant pink tubular flowers attract hummingbirds and are highly ornamental; the vine tolerates light frost better than most passion fruits and grows best in mild, humid conditions rather than the hot lowland tropics. It has escaped cultivation in Hawaii and New Zealand where it is invasive — deadhead spent fruits to prevent self-seeding when grown in mild climates.
sources: Hawaii Division of Forestry and Wildlife Invasive Species Lists
